speaker: interviewer
text: --five. Interview with Henry Coleman. You were saying you decided to leave Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Before we left Vienna, we came into Salzburg. I don't know why, but they shipped us to Salzburg, and we stayed a few weeks in Salzburg. And then from Salzburg, we decided we going to Germany, because we couldn't-- we heard so many stories. Being in Vienna, when my wife was sick in the hospital, a nurse in the hospital-- a young girl, a Jewish girl-- told us a story. She had a Jewish father and a German mother. She told us a story that the mother sent her own husband to Dachau. In other words, she went to the Germans and told them that her husband is Jewish, and he was sent to Dachau and perished there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the mother was trying, with all means, to get together with two girls. One was working in the [INAUDIBLE] hospital as a nurse, and she was trying with all-- all their-- her means to get together with their daughters. But they don't-- they don't want nothing to do with her, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I felt-- I mean, the story itself was so heartbreaking, you know, but how-- but I felt very good about it that their-- their-- her own children would-- wouldn't do anything to do with her because what she did to her-- to their father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, we went to Salzburg. And from Salzburg, we-- they brought us in trucks, with covered trucks, because, you know, it was all-- all was illegal. We had to go through the Brenner Pass to Italy, through the mountains of Italy, to get into Germany, to Bavaria. And we had to sit still there, you know, in these trucks, you know, and no-- no breathing or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came into [INAUDIBLE], which is Bavaria, which is near the Bavarian Alps. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we stayed there. My wife had cousins there in that camp. We stayed there a short while, and then we went to Munich. We're shipped to Munich. We didn't go there, we were shipped to Munich. And we were in, like, a-- again, in a army, like, camp or what, in town, in the middle of Munich. And then they shipped us to a camp, which is called [INAUDIBLE], which is near Foehrenwald. Foehrenwald was already a big DP camp, and this was a camp in a forest. It was also an army camp, and there were barracks inside the forest. There was a brand new camp, because it was-- everything was new formed, everything. And they brought in about a few hundred people there. Maybe 200, 300 people. But I-- I didn't like to stay there. And I didn't want to stay there, because my wife was always sick, you know. I figured I'd rather just stay near a bigger town, you know. The only small town near there was [INAUDIBLE], which is closer to Foehrenwald than to us. We were closer to Garmisch. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was traveling to different camps, and I was registering myself in Deggendorf, and Eichstatt. But then I decided, you know, because I felt it may be good for my wife, because I've seen the other camps were very crowded, and this camp that we were in, the air was good because there was, you know, a lot of pine trees. So I figured maybe it would be good for my wife, and I remained in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed there from 1947-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 47. Of course, I was in different places from 1945 to 1947. And we stayed there till 1948. And then when Israel became a state, independent state, they brought in a lot of DP-- you know, DPs to our camp, and they were inoculated with injection for, I don't know, whatever, you know. And my wife was a nurse herself. She worked in the ambulance, and she was giving the injections to people before they went on the-- on the boat. And after awhile, they closed that camp, and they moved us to Neu Freimann, which is near Munich. And they did the same thing over there. A lot of-- it was like a transit camp for people who went to Israel. And my wife was still working there with another lady. We shared a house. We already had our own house. We were upstairs-- we had a bedroom upstairs, she had a bedroom downstairs, and we shared the kitchen. And we had already a garden, you know, with fruits, you know, vegetables. And we had a cow where we had milk, fresh milk. But that lasted only till about 1949. Then they closed that camp, too. And naturally, the Germans already moved in to pick through their possessions. And we moved into Munich proper. And we got a room on [INAUDIBLE] Strasse in Munich. On the third floor. A walk-up. But my wife was sick. She was in Munich in the hospital. And she was-- I don't know-- sick, you know, continuously. I went to doctors, to professors, you know. She had a-- a-- she had different things, you know, with her wrong, you know. And then we decided-- our family in Australia sent us immigration papers to Australia. But they made us so much-- so many difficulties, you know. First of all, we-- if-- if I have a trade. So I went to [INAUDIBLE], and I learned how to be a tailor. I was no good at it, but I learned a little bit, and they gave me a-- a paper with a picture, which I still have. And I had to go to [INAUDIBLE], because there were no Australian consulate in Munich. And they asked you all kind of questions. If you're sick. If you have tuberculosis or you have venereal diseases, you know. And all kinds of questions, you know. Then, all of a sudden-- then they approved our visa. And we already had a passport. That was before the Jewish holidays, the High Holidays. And we already packed everything up and were sitting on our-- on our crates and boxes in that room. All of a sudden, we get a telegram from the HIAS. The German government-- well the government-- the German government was still in charge of Germany. There was no German government in-- in-- in-- in state yet. You know, there was no German government. It was the military government. And we get a notice of the German government-- from the military government that our passport was revoked. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't know why. I was never-- except I was-- I figured may I was in concentration camp. I was never imprisoned. I was never-- there was a lot of black market dealing around. I was never involved in black market dealings. I couldn't find-- for no money in the world, I couldn't find out from the German-- from the military government-- American military government why my passport was revoked. I went to the HIAS, and they couldn't find out either. After a couple months, I was noticed it was a mistake in the name. But since then, I decided I don't want to go no more to Australia. And I had family here in the United States. My father's four brothers were here, and families. And they sent me an affidavit a long time before that, you know. But I just let it slide. But then I renewed my application. And in September, 1951, I arrived in New York. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were in the HIAS, on Lafayette Street, in New York for two, three weeks, because my family wanted me to take in with them. And I don't want to-- I don't want to be a-- a-- a-- both-- you know. You know, I don't want to be-- I don't I want to be bothered-- I didn't want them to be bothered with me. Or I didn't want to be a-- a burden to my family, because I didn't know them that well. I could hardly even-- I spoke with them. Some of them-- my uncle spoke Yiddish, but the rest of the family-- the rest of the sons and daughters couldn't speak a word of Yiddish. I spoke a few-- a little bit of English, I could. Conversing-- I learned English. I could write and read, but I couldn't converse. It was very hard for me to converse. But it took me, like, two, three weeks, and I conversed. And I stayed in HIAS. Then I took a job on 24th Street, between Fifth and Sixth Avenue. I worked a few weeks there. I didn't like it. I changed my job again. And then I didn't like that job either. And I was working for a supermarket. And then I opened up a food store myself. I lived-- funny thing is, I lived in my family-- I lived-- I had an apartment in Brooklyn, in Bensonhurst, on 64th Street and 16th Avenue. But I had only one room. An Italian divided up his-- his apartment, so I had one and a little kitchen. But I had the trains going there, three-- three-- fourteen times a day. And I couldn't sleep. I couldn't'-- couldn't breathe. The Sea Beach, the Brighton Beach, the West End.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I was-- found out there's an apartment to be gotten in Manhattan, in Washington Heights on 152nd Street. And I went there for an interview. The owner of the building was a name-- a man by the name Goodman. And he had a hotel on West End Street between West End Avenue and Riverside Drive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went there for an interview, but the-- the-- the thing was then, you know, that I had to pay underneath the table money. So he asked me to pay $500, and I did. But a funny thing happened. My aunt called me, and she asked me if I was there for the interview and if I got the apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: I says, yes. Then she asked me, did you pay him any money? I said, yes, $500. She said, go right back-- he is your cousin-- and tell him I said to give you back the money.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back there, and he gave me back the $500. He reduced my rent, and I lived on 152nd Street near Riverside Drive. And they did everything for me, you know. They helped me a lot. I mean, whatever they could, they did. And I stayed there till the area became a little inconvenient. Then I moved up to 204th Street, which is called Inwood. I stayed there for awhile. Then I bought a house in Flushing, because already was working and making money. I bought a house on 167th Street, between Union Turnpike and 73rd Avenue. And I lived there till 1961. No, till-- pardon me, till 1991. But my wife passed away in 1989. [SOBBING] She was sick for 10 years. And I knew she had a very hard condition, but I kept her comfortable for 10 years. Doctors told me that-- I tried Texas, you know, with that doctor in Texas who did the surgery-- heart operations. I had a cousin in Texas, a doctor. And I-- I went to all kinds of hospitals. And I thought maybe she can have surgery. But they told me it's too risky, because her main artery is deteriorated. And they have no real way to attach the-- the-- you know, the bypass. So I kept her home, as comfortable as I could. She lived for 10 years. From 1974 till 19-- more than 10 years. From 1974 to 1989. And she passed away, and she's buried in New York. I-- I've just been, last week, to the cemetery for a visit. She is buried in Beth David [INAUDIBLE] in Elmont. And I was alone for a year and a half, two years. And then my current wife's husband, which we were friend for 40 years, we knew each other. We were very good friends. Her husband passed away all of a sudden. He was never sick a day in his life. I went to his funeral in-- in Brookline. And then after a year and a half, two years, we decided to get married. And I sold my house, and I moved to Brookline. And my daughter moves instant to Florida. And she has a third girl. And my stepdaughter has two sons. And she had also, a girl just a month older than my daughter's girl. And my wife has also another son in Washington. He was here a week ago. He's an attorney in Washington, DC. Has two girls. We are now a very happy family. And we hope to live as long as we can. [SOBBING] And this is my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's 50 years later, is there any message you would like to leave for your grand--

speaker: interviewee
text: Speak a little louder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's 50 years later, is there any message you'd like to leave for your children, your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: We talk to our grandchildren. I was very instrumental that my daughter put the kids into Hebrew school. It's a hardship on them, because they're in a very affluent area in Boca Raton, and they really cannot afford to stay there, to live there, but, you know, children don't realize sometimes what they're doing. But they're still going to Hebrew school, and they're very good at it. And my stepdaughter, her husband is American. And he really knows very little about being Jewish or Judaism. But I was also instrumental, and the kids-- she has one boy, very bright, which is eight years old, he's going to Hebrew school. The youngest one, which is going to be six, Sammy, is going to go to Hebrew school. And the little girl, I hope-- she's going to be two years in October-- I hope. But I'm always-- they know. They come, the holidays, to my house. We, you know, conduct services. We-- Passover and all other holidays, Friday night. I was always observant, but I became more so observant [SOBBING] since my wife passed away. My wife now was never observant, neither was her husband. But she learned. She learned now to-- to the ways of being observant. She makes mistakes here and there with the, you know, with the dishes, with the flatware, with the dairy and the milk.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the kids now know that when they come to my house, they know they cannot have dairy after they ate meat. Or they cannot have-- they like-- the little girl, you know, my son-- my stepson was here-- very nice man. Very-- so delicate, so nice, so good. And the younger girl said, I would like to have-- he asked her, what would you like to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, oh, a cheeseburger. She says, oh, I forgot. I cannot have a cheeseburger in my-- in my-- in my zayde's house. So they know the differences. And they know. They always ask me about the number, and I explain to them in as little ways as I can. Because you cannot-- to a child, you cannot tell a story like this, because it's beyond, you know, their imagination. They cannot conceive something like this. They cannot understand something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I tell them in-- in my own ways. I tell them, there were some bad people who were killing other people, who killed my father, my mother, my brothers, my sisters, your fathers, your grandfathers, your grandmother, your-- your aunts. So the best way I can. But they know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hope they'll-- they'll remember it, because, you know, we always say in our daily prayers, remember what Amalek did to you. What Amalek means the-- in prehistorical times when the Jews were enslaved. Well, we called also, the Germans, Amalekem now. But I always emphasize to my children, you know, never forget. I don't ask them to be cruel or to hate people, but always remember what they did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And many times, you know, when you watch TV, you had Bosnia. You had Biafra. You had other areas, you know. And I tell you, for-- if-- honestly, my God's honest truth, I said, I don't-- I feel sorry for them, but I-- I don't feel any compassion. Because the world did so much harm to our-- to our Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They could have saved a lot of Jews. Hitler didn't want the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: He told America, to England, take them. We don't want them. You want them? Take them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't put up a finger to-- to save the Jews. In fact, they contributed a lot to the destruction of Jews by just allowing the St. Louis to land here. Or you read so many things now that are coming out in books, what they did, what the State Department did, what England did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, in my heart, I don't feel no compassion. My heart became like a stone, because I always watch. I says, what happened to the Jewish children? [INAUDIBLE] million of Jewish children? Where were those-- all those do-gooders? What-- what-- what did they do for the Jewish children? Why didn't they save some of them? Why they let them butcher so atrociously? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So, you know, at times, you know, you become hard. But-- but, you know, I always say to my wife, I saw so many things, so many terrible things. But even now, when I saw somebody cutting a finger, when I see blood, I get, like, a-- like a thing going from my head to my feet. You know, I feel sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many times I was in New York, I was at the subway, and I saw some poor people. I saw once a woman in the winter, barefoot. And she-- she was not-- I don't think she was normal. But she ran away. And I ran after her, and I gave her a dollar.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I give to charity, you know. A lot of people don't think-- don't think that way, you know? It's unfortunately that people don't think that way, because I saw what happened to money, to wealth. Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: It went up in flames. You couldn't buy, for a diamond, a piece of bread. And people forget. I don't forget. I remember. And I want my children to remember, too. And I know my son, my stepson, gives to charity. His kids go to Hebrew school, like I said. My stepdaughter, gives a lot. Her husband, which is a dentist, he's-- he's very-- he's very well off. He has several dental offices. He lives in a very wealthy area. He's wealthy himself, but he does give a lot to Israel. I went to Israel, you know. I'm in Israel very often. I went to a Yeshiva, a-- a home for children. I left-- I made a plaque there for my wife, and I left $1,500. I went to another hospital in Jerusalem. I left $1,000. I mean, I'm not a wealthy man, but as much as I could, I help. Whenever I can, I help. Because, you know, the main-- the mainstay of our religion is tzedakah, is learning, is Torah. And people, unfortunately, forget and forgot about those things. But there are plenty people who I know contribute and give and help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Coleman, could you please introduce your wife to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Irene Ballor Coleman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather, which he was born in Rypin and died there. That's all I can tell about him, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he your mother or father's father?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is my father's father. This is my mother. Her name is Nycha Kalma. Her maiden name was Kolton. She was born in the late 1800s, early 1900s. She married in 1913. She had six children-- four boys and two girls. During the war and before the war, she was very active in Zionist organizations, collecting monies for poor people, for poor girls who had to get married or wanted to get married, and very active in a lot of other organizations. Also, in some, you know, orthodox organizations. She has-- had was very orthodox at home and at the outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the war, we-- we had to escape from our hometown to Mlawa, where they formed a ghetto. And from there, after a while, there-- we were shipped out to Miedzyrzec, which is Mezrich in Jewish. And she was-- sorry? How old is she here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here she is about 22. This is my father, Israel Kalma. Born also in the late 1900s-- 1800s, pardon me. And he was married in 1913 to my mother. And they had-- their first son was born in 1914. He was very orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is he here?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's about 27, 28.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father's family. My uncle, my father's brother, uncle Abraham; his wife, Sarah. His-- my aunt, Viola. Her daughter, Rivka. Her son, Abraham. My uncle, Baruch Mendel, and his wife, Hannah. And my uncle's-- Abraham's wife, Sarah, also. The picture was taken in the early 1900s in my hometown, Rypin. This is my older sister, Bella. Born in 1920 in Rypin. On the right side. On the left side is her friend, school friend, Schlachter, which was taken somewhere in the middle of the 1900s. About 1930, in Rypin. My brother Zalman David, which is on the right side. Born in 1918. And those are his two friends. The picture was taken in Rypin in 1922. This is my oldest brother, Moshe, and his girlfriend, Mindel. Taken in front of our house in 1933. And this is our dog. Figel, was his name. This is my wife, Jean Coleman. Born in 1924, January 27, 1924. And the picture was taken in 1984. This is my current wife, Irene Ballor Coleman. We got married in 1991, in New York.
